main_damage_causes: Removal of the St Mungo's scene (GoF) eliminates the emotional foundation of Neville's entire arc and severs the personal meaning of his confrontations with Bellatrix in OotP and DH

Systematic cutting of courage-building micro-arcs in PS/SS (Leg-Locker curse, 'worth twelve of Malfoy,' active participation in the third-floor adventure) reduces Neville to comic relief and makes his eventual heroism feel unearned

Consistent use of Neville as slapstick physical comedy (flying lesson, Mandrake fainting) at the expense of the books' portrayal of him as anxious but genuinely trying, flattening his characterisation across multiple films

damaging_scenes.book_baseline: Neville falls over on his way to the stool, the Hat takes a long time to decide, and he runs off still wearing it amid gales of laughter — establishing him as clumsy, anxious, but genuinely placed in Gryffindor after deliberation.
damaging_scenes.film_portrayal: Neville's sorting is entirely cut. His only moment is catching Trevor and apologising to McGonagall. The Hat's deliberation and the laughter beat are absent.
damaging_scenes.explanation: The book's sorting beat establishes Neville's clumsiness and anxiety while also showing the Hat genuinely chose him — important groundwork for his arc. Its absence removes early characterisation.

damaging_scenes.scene: PS/SS Film - Flying lesson / Remembrall incident
damaging_scenes.book_baseline: Malfoy snatches the Remembrall from Neville at breakfast; Neville reports it to McGonagall; during the flying lesson Neville's broom rises before the whistle and he falls, breaking his wrist. Harry then chases Malfoy to recover the Remembrall.
damaging_scenes.film_portrayal: The breakfast theft is cut entirely. During the flying lesson Neville's broom goes haywire in a slapstick extended sequence — he flies into a wall, hangs from a torch bracket, and falls. Malfoy picks up the Remembrall from the ground after Neville falls. The incident becomes pure physical comedy rather than Malfoy's deliberate bullying of a vulnerable boy.
damaging_scenes.explanation: The book uses the Remembrall theft to show Neville as a target of calculated cruelty and Harry's protectiveness of him. The film replaces this with broad slapstick, reducing Neville to comic relief and softening the bullying dynamic.

damaging_scenes.scene: PS/SS Film - Leg-Locker Curse / 'You're worth twelve of Malfoy'
damaging_scenes.book_baseline: Malfoy uses the Leg-Locker Curse on Neville outside the library. Neville hobbles into the common room. Harry gives him a Chocolate Frog and tells him 'You're worth twelve of Malfoy.' Neville then stands up to Malfoy at the Quidditch match, telling him 'I'm worth twelve of you.' This is a small but complete arc of courage-building.
damaging_scenes.film_portrayal: This entire sequence is absent from the film.
damaging_scenes.explanation: This is the first micro-arc of Neville's courage development. Harry's encouragement directly produces Neville's public stand against Malfoy. Cutting it removes the earliest evidence that Neville can grow, and severs a meaningful Harry–Neville bond that foreshadows the finale.

damaging_scenes.scene: PS/SS Film - Night-time adventure / trophy room chase
damaging_scenes.book_baseline: Neville joins Harry, Ron, and Hermione on the night-time adventure, panics and causes a suit of armour to crash, and is present for the discovery of Fluffy. He is an active (if frightened) participant.
damaging_scenes.film_portrayal: Neville is not part of the group that goes to the third floor. He appears only briefly outside the common room and is Petrified by Hermione so the trio can leave. He plays no role in the Fluffy discovery.
damaging_scenes.explanation: In the book Neville's presence in the adventure — and his terror — is part of his characterisation as someone who tries despite fear. The film reduces him to an obstacle the heroes must neutralise, which is actively contrary to his arc.

damaging_scenes.scene: PS/SS Film - End-of-year feast / ten points
damaging_scenes.book_baseline: Dumbledore awards Neville ten points for 'standing up to his friends,' which wins Gryffindor the House Cup. Neville is described as white with shock and disappearing under a pile of people hugging him. He had never won so much as a point before.
damaging_scenes.film_portrayal: The scene is present and the ten points are awarded, but the emotional weight of Neville never having won a point before is absent because the film has not built his arc of failure and self-doubt across the year.
damaging_scenes.explanation: The payoff exists but lands with reduced force because the preceding arc beats (Leg-Locker, 'worth twelve of Malfoy,' active participation in the adventure) were cut. The moment is present but hollowed.

damaging_scenes.book_baseline: Neville is one of many students in the Mandrake lesson; his clumsiness is incidental.
damaging_scenes.film_portrayal: Neville faints dramatically when Professor Sprout pulls the Mandrake, and is left lying on the ground while the class continues. Played for laughs.
damaging_scenes.explanation: Minor but consistent pattern of using Neville as a sight gag. The book does not have him faint here.

damaging_scenes.book_baseline: Snape says Longbottom 'causes devastation with the simplest spells' and pairs him with Justin Finch-Fletchley. Both end up on the floor after their duel. Neville's face goes pink at Snape's public humiliation.
damaging_scenes.film_portrayal: Snape's line is redirected: he says Weasley's broken wand would send Potter to the hospital wing, and pairs Harry with Malfoy instead. Neville is not singled out at the Duelling Club at all.
damaging_scenes.explanation: A small loss, but the book's version reinforces Snape's specific targeting of Neville as a pattern of cruelty, which matters for the Boggart lesson payoff.

damaging_scenes.book_baseline: Neville leads the class against the Boggart, his fear of Snape is revealed, he successfully casts Riddikulus twice, earns ten points, and the class erupts in applause. Lupin's choice to give Neville the lead is a deliberate act of mentorship that visibly transforms him.
damaging_scenes.film_portrayal: The scene is present and largely faithful. Neville succeeds. However, the film compresses Lupin's whispered instruction and Neville's visible terror before acting, and cuts the explicit ten-points award to Neville.
damaging_scenes.explanation: Mostly faithful; minor compression reduces the explicit reward beat but the core characterisation survives.

damaging_scenes.scene: PoA Film - Password list / Sirius Black break-in
damaging_scenes.book_baseline: Neville writes down the passwords and loses the list, enabling Sirius Black's entry. He is publicly shamed by McGonagall, receives a Howler from his grandmother, and is banned from Hogsmeade. The book shows his genuine remorse and the social cost he pays.
damaging_scenes.film_portrayal: The scene is present: Neville admits to writing down the passwords. However, the Howler from his grandmother and the extended social ostracism are absent, reducing the consequence and emotional weight.
damaging_scenes.explanation: The book uses this incident to show Neville's relationship with his formidable grandmother and the real cost of his forgetfulness. The film keeps the confession but strips the aftermath, losing characterisation of both Neville and Augusta Longbottom.

damaging_scenes.scene: GoF Film - Moody's DADA class / Cruciatus Curse demonstration
damaging_scenes.book_baseline: Neville raises his hand to name the Cruciatus Curse. Moody recognises his name and stares at him. Neville watches the spider being tortured with white-knuckled horror — his parents were tortured by this curse. Moody takes him for tea and lends him a Herbology book, telling him Professor Sprout praised his talent. Neville is found afterwards staring at the wall, clearly traumatised.
damaging_scenes.film_portrayal: Neville names the Cruciatus Curse and is visibly distressed during the demonstration. Hermione notices. The aftermath — Moody taking Neville aside, the Herbology book, the tactful mention of Sprout's praise — is cut entirely.
damaging_scenes.explanation: The book's aftermath is crucial: it shows Neville's trauma has a specific cause (his parents), that Moody/Crouch Jr. is aware of this and manipulates it, and that Neville rarely hears he is good at anything. Cutting it removes depth from both Neville's psychology and the Crouch Jr. subplot.

damaging_scenes.scene: GoF Film - Yule Ball / Neville asks Hermione
damaging_scenes.book_baseline: Neville asks Hermione to the ball; she declines because she already has a partner. He then asks Ginny, who accepts. Ron's reaction ('who would?') is a moment of casual cruelty that the narrative does not endorse.
damaging_scenes.film_portrayal: Neville and Ginny attend the ball together and Neville is shown dancing (badly, treading on Ginny's feet). The fact that he asked Hermione first and was turned down is absent.
damaging_scenes.explanation: Minor loss. The book beat shows Neville's social courage in asking; the film skips to the result.

damaging_scenes.scene: GoF Film - St Mungo's / Neville's parents
damaging_scenes.book_baseline: Harry, Ron, Hermione, and Ginny encounter Neville visiting his parents at St Mungo's. Neville is mortified. His mother gives him a Drooble's wrapper; he quietly pockets it. Mrs Longbottom publicly shames him for not telling his friends. The scene is devastating and intimate.
damaging_scenes.film_portrayal: This scene does not appear in the GoF film. The revelation of Neville's parents' fate is delivered instead via Sirius showing Harry the Order of the Phoenix photograph at King's Cross, naming Frank and Alice Longbottom as having 'suffered a fate worse than death.' Neville is not present.
damaging_scenes.explanation: The St Mungo's scene is the emotional core of Neville's entire arc. It explains his fear of Bellatrix, his motivation in the Department of Mysteries and the Battle of Hogwarts, and his complex relationship with his grandmother. Replacing it with a secondhand mention by Sirius — with Neville absent — strips him of agency in his own backstory and removes the most humanising scene he has in the entire series.

damaging_scenes.scene: OotP Film - Department of Mysteries battle
damaging_scenes.book_baseline: Neville fights throughout with a broken nose, carries Hermione, refuses to give the prophecy to Dolohov ('Whaddever you do, Harry, don'd gib it to him!'), jabs Hermione's wand into a Death Eater's eyehole to save Harry from choking, and is Cruciated by Bellatrix. His confrontation with Bellatrix — whose name he knows — is charged with personal meaning.
damaging_scenes.film_portrayal: Neville is present and fights. However, the broken-nose dialogue, the extended sequence of him carrying Hermione, and the specific moment of him saving Harry by jabbing the eyehole are compressed or absent. His recognition of Bellatrix and its personal significance is not conveyed because the St Mungo's scene was cut.
damaging_scenes.explanation: Because the St Mungo's scene was cut, Neville's confrontation with Bellatrix has no emotional resonance. The film shows him fighting bravely but the audience cannot understand why Bellatrix is specifically significant to him, gutting the scene's meaning.

damaging_scenes.scene: DH2 Film - Neville's speech / Nagini / Sorting Hat
damaging_scenes.book_baseline: Neville steps forward, defies Voldemort, is set on fire inside the Sorting Hat, breaks free of the Body-Bind, draws the sword of Gryffindor from the Hat, and decapitates Nagini — the last Horcrux — in a single fluid moment. He had been entrusted by Harry with the task of killing the snake. The moment is the culmination of his entire arc.
damaging_scenes.film_portrayal: Neville gives a speech to the assembled survivors, spits at Voldemort, draws the sword, and later decapitates Nagini. The sequence is present and heroic. However, the specific prior scene of Harry privately asking Neville to kill the snake (trusting him as a backup in case Ron and Hermione cannot) is absent, so Neville acts on his own initiative rather than fulfilling a trust placed in him by Harry.
damaging_scenes.explanation: The film's version is heroic but removes the Harry–Neville bond of trust that makes the book moment so resonant. In the book Harry specifically chooses Neville as his backup; in the film Neville simply acts. The relationship dimension is lost.
